5. HEALTH
5.1. DISEASES AND RELATED DEVELOPMENTS
5.1.1. TUBERCULOSIS (TB)
Why in the News?
According to the WHO Global TB Report 2025, India’s TB incidence (new cases emerging each year) reduced
by 21%.
About Tuberculosis (TB)
• A contagious airborne disease caused by a bacterium called Mycobacterium tuberculosis.
• Types based on site of Infection:
o Pulmonary TB: Most common form affecting the lungs, and is contagious spreading through airborne
droplets through coughing or sneezing.
o Extrapulmonary TB: Occurs outside the lungs, affecting organs like lymph nodes, bones, brain,
kidneys, or the pleura, less contagious and spreads within the body from lungs.
o Spinal TB (Pott’s disease): Affects the spine and can cause severe back pain and mobility issues.
• Types based on Response to Drugs:
o Drug-sensitive TB: This form of TB responds well to the standard first-line anti-TB medicines such as
isoniazid and rifampicin.
o Drug-Resistant TB: Some forms of TB do not respond to standard medicines.
 Multi Drug Resistant (MDR-TB): Form of TB that does not respond to rifampicin and isoniazid, the
two most effective first-line TB drugs.
 Extensively Drug-Resistant (XDR-TB): In this type of TB, the stronger medicines may not work.
5.1.2. HUMAN IMMUNODEFICIENCY VIRUS (HIV)
Why in the News?
US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approves new Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV) prevention drug
Lenacapavir.
About Lenacapavir
• Lenacapavir is an antiretroviral medicine that is used for HIV prevention as a Pre-Exposure Prophylaxis or
PrEP.
o PrEP is a medication that can reduce the risk of HIV infection for individuals who are HIV-negative
but are at risk of contracting the virus.
About HIV
• A virus that attacks the body’s immune system.
• Mechanism: HIV damages the immune system by infecting and destroying CD4 cells (CD4 T lymphocytes),
a type of white blood cells.
• Transmission: Body fluids of an infected person, including blood, breast milk, semen and vaginal fluids.
o It can also spread from a mother to her baby.
• Treatment: HIV can be prevented and treated with antiretroviral therapy (ART).
o Untreated HIV can progress to acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (AIDS).
5.1.3. OTHER DISEASES IN NEWS
5.1.3.1. TRACHOMA
India was awarded the Certificate of Elimination of Trachoma as a Public Health Problem by the WHO.

• India was declared the 3rd (after Nepal and Myanmar) Southeast Asian region country to have eliminated
Trachoma.
About Trachoma
• An eye disease caused by the bacterium Chlamydia trachomatis.
o It is categorized as a Neglected Tropical Disease by the WHO.
• Impact: Irreversible blindness
• Spread: Personal contact or through flies
• Indian Initiative: Trachoma control program integrated under the National Program for Control of
Blindness and Visual Impairment (NPCBVI)
5.1.3.2. HEPATITIS
WHO has classified Hepatitis D as Cancer-Causing, like Hepatitis B and C.
• Hepatitis D is an incompetent virus that requires hepatitis B virus (HBV) for replication.
• This means hepatitis D only affects people who already have hepatitis B either simultaneously or
sequentially.
About Hepatitis Virus
• Viral hepatitis (types A, B, C, D, and E) are major causes of acute liver infection.
• However, only hepatitis B, C, and D can lead to chronic infections with a higher risk of liver cirrhosis,
failure or cancer.
• Vaccine Availability
o There is no vaccine for Hepatitis C virus.
o Hepatitis B vaccines provide protection from HDV infection.
o Havisure is India’s first indigenous Hepatitis A vaccine.
5.1.3.3. MPOX (MONKEYPOX)
WHO chief says mpox outbreak in Africa is no longer a global health emergency.
About Mpox
• Type: Viral zoonotic disease caused by Monkeypox virus (Orthopoxvirus genus).
• Transmission: Close contact with infected people, animals, or contaminated objects.
• Symptoms: Fever, headache, muscle aches, fatigue, swollen lymph nodes.
• Global Concern: Declared a Public Health Emergency of International Concern (PHEIC) by WHO.
• There is no proven treatment for Mpox.
• Diagnostic Test: RT-PCR (Reverse Transcription Polymerase Chain Reaction)
5.1.3.4. HUMAN PAPILLOMAVIRUS (HPV)
India’s first indigenously developed HPV test kits for cervical cancer screening validated by DBT.
About HPV
• A common sexually transmitted infection which can affect the skin, genital area and throat.
• In most cases the immune system clears HPV from the body.
• Persistent infection with high-risk HPV can cause abnormal cells to develop, which go on to become
cancer.
o Cervical cancer is the most common type of cancer caused by HPV.
• Treatment: There is currently no treatment for HPV infection.

45 AHMEDABAD | BENGALURU | BHOPAL | CHANDIGARH | DELHI | GUWAHATI | HYDERABAD | JAIPUR | JODHPUR | LUCKNOW | PRAYAGRAJ | PUNE | RANCHI ©Vision IAS
5.2. NOBEL PRIZE IN PHYSIOLOGY OR MEDICINE 2025
Why in the News?
Mary E. Brunkow, Fred Ramsdell and Shimon Sakaguchi have been awarded Nobel Prize in Physiology or
Medicine 2025 for their discoveries concerning peripheral immune tolerance.
More on the News
• Our immune system protects the body from harmful pathogens like viruses, bacteria, and fungi.
• However, it must also make sure not to attack its own cells — this balance is very important for healthy
body function.
• The immune system uses two types of tolerance to achieve this — central tolerance and peripheral
tolerance.
Central Tolerance
• This process happens in the thymus, a small gland in the lymphatic system that produces and trains T cells
(a type of white blood cell).
o T cells protect our body from infections.
• During training, the thymus removes self-reactive T cells (harmful cells).
• Some of these self-reactive T cells escape from the thymus and enter the body’s circulation and tissues,
known as the periphery.
Peripheral Tolerance
• Additional control is needed to prevent escaped self-reactive T cells from attacking the body.
o Here, Regulatory T cells (Treg cells) play a key role.
• Regulatory T cells identify self-reactive T cells and stop them from attacking our own tissues.
• In this way, Treg cells act as a security guard.
Role of the FOXP3 Gene
• Mary Brunkow and Fred Ramsdell discovered the FOXP3 gene, which controls the development and
function of regulatory T cells.
o If there is a mutation in the FOXP3 gene, regulatory T cells do not form properly.
• This can cause a rare autoimmune disease called IPEX, where the immune system attacks the body’s own
tissues.
5.3. ANTIMICROBIAL RESISTANCE (AMR)
Why in the News?
The World Health Organization (WHO) released the Global Antibiotic Resistance Surveillance Report 2025.
What is meant by Antimicrobial Resistance (AMR)?
• AMR occurs when bacteria, viruses, fungi, and parasites evolve to withstand the drugs designed to kill
them.

• AMR is a natural process that happens over time through genetic changes in pathogens.
o However, its
emergence
and spread are
accelerated
due to several
anthropogenic
factors.
• Strains of bacteria,
viruses, parasites,
and fungi that are
resistant to most
antimicrobials are
called Superbugs.
• Spread through
vertical gene
transfer during bacterial division and through horizontal gene transfer by exchange of resistance genes
among microbes.
• WHO recognized AMR as one of the top ten global health threats.
o It is often described as a silent pandemic.
5.4. FIXED-DOSE COMBINATION (FDC) DRUGS
Why in the News?
Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation (CDSCO) has directed all states and Union Territories to stop
the manufacture, sale, and distribution of 35 unapproved FDC drugs.
About Fixed-Dose Combination (FDC) Drugs
• FDC refer to products containing one or more active ingredients used for a particular indication(s).
o Active Ingredient is the biologically active component of a drug product (tablet, capsule, cream,
injectable) that produces the intended effects.
• FDCs are considered new drug (under NDCT) Rules, 2019) when it meets two conditions:
o It combines two or more drugs already approved for individual use in a fixed ratio.
o It alters the ratio of drugs in an already approved FDC, along with making changes to its claims.
Why FDC drugs are Preferred?
• Better Therapeutic Outcomes: Enhanced synergistic effects between combined drugs (when rationally
selected).
• Improved Patient Compliance: Reduces the pill burden.
• Reduced Cost of Therapy: Combined products may lower costs compared to purchasing multiple
individual drugs.
Regulation of Pharma Products in India
• Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940 & Drugs and Cosmetics Rules, 1945: Governs import, manufacture,
distribution, and sale of drugs and cosmetics in India.
• Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation (CDSCO): Under Directorate General of Health Services,
Ministry of Health & Family Welfare is National Regulatory Authority of India.
o Under Drugs and Cosmetics Act, CDSCO is responsible for approval of Drugs, Conduct of Clinical
Trials, laying down the standards for Drugs, control over the quality of imported Drugs in country.
• State Drug Regulatory Authorities: Empowered to grant licenses, conduct inspections, and ensure
compliance with drug laws at state level.
• National Pharmaceutical Pricing Authority (NPPA): Enforces provisions of Drugs (Prices Control) Order.
• Good Manufacturing Practices (GMP): Schedule M of Drugs and Cosmetics Rules prescribes Good
Manufacturing Practices (GMP), which align with WHO guidelines.

SHRESTH Initiative
• Union Health Ministry launched State Health Regulatory Excellence Index (SHRESTH), a national
initiative to benchmark and strengthens state drug regulatory systems.
• About SHRESTH
o Proposed by the CDSCO.
o Aim: To drive improvements in the performance of state drug regulatory authorities across India,
ensuring drug safety and quality standards are consistently met.
5.5. VACCINE IN NEWS
5.5.1. ADFALCIVAX VACCINE
Why in the News?
Government of India has licensed private firms to produce its first indigenous multi-stage malaria vaccine,
AdFalciVax.
More on the News
• Vaccine is being developed through ICMR's Regional Medical Research Centre, Bhubaneswar and
National Institute of Malaria Research in partnership with Department of Biotechnology-National
Institute of Immunology.
About AdFalciVax Vaccine
• Dual-Stage Targeting of Plasmodium falciparum (Deadliest malaria parasite Species): Unlike existing
vaccines (RTS, S/AS01 (Mosquirix) and R21/Matrix-M) that target only one life stage; it targets two critical
stages in parasite’s lifecycle i.e. Pre-erythrocytic (liver) stage and Transmission (sexual) stage.

• Recombinant Chimeric Vaccine: A chimeric vaccine refers to vaccine that combines antigens from
different stages of a parasite's life cycle into a single immunogen.
About Malaria
• Life-threatening disease caused by Plasmodium parasites.
o Malaria mostly spreads to people through bites of some infected female Anopheles mosquitoes.
• Five Plasmodium species cause malaria in humans: P. falciparum, P. vivax, P. malariae, P. ovale, P.
knowlesi.
5.5.2. ONCOLYTIC & PERSONALIZED MRNA VACCINES
Why in the News?
Russia is developing oncolytic vaccine Enteromix and Personalized messenger RNA (mRNA) cancer vaccines
for cancer treatment.
About Oncolytic Vaccine Enteromix
• An Oncolytic vaccine uses oncolytic viruses (OVs) to directly kill cancer cells and stimulate an anti-
tumor immune response.
o Enteromix is based on a combination of four
non-pathogenic viruses that have the ability to
destroy malignant cells and simultaneously
activate the patient's antitumor immunity.
About Personalized mRNA Vaccines
• Personalized Vaccine: Based on the genetic
analysis of each patient's cancer cells.
• mRNA vaccines: The vaccine contains a synthetic
mRNA strand that encodes a specific protein from
the virus
o mRNA delivers genetic material, encapsulated in lipid nanoparticles, into body to prompt protein
production that match up with parts of pathogen called antigens.
o E.g., the mRNA vaccine for COVID-19 directed cells to produce copies of a protein on the outside of the
coronavirus known as the spike protein.
Related News
Self-amplifying mRNA (saRNA) Vaccine
• Gennova Biopharmaceuticals is developing a pioneering self-amplifying mRNA (saRNA) vaccine
candidate against the Nipah virus, as part of expanded funding from the Coalition for Epidemic
Preparedness Innovations (CEPI).
• saRNA vaccines work by giving the body instructions to replicate mRNA needed to create the desired
antigen, potentially enhancing the immune response with lower doses compared to existing mRNA
vaccines.
5.6. OTHER IMPORTANT NEWS/DEVELOPMENTS
5.6.1. WHO PANDEMIC AGREEMENT
WHO member states held their first meeting of the Intergovernmental Working Group (IGWG) on the WHO
Pandemic Agreement.
About WHO Pandemic Agreement
• Second international legal agreement negotiated under Article 19 of the WHO Constitution (first being
the WHO Framework Convention on Tobacco Control, 2003).

• Key Highlights of the Agreement
o Strengthen Pandemic prevention and surveillance: As per International Health Regulations (IHR),
2005.
o Pathogen Access and Benefit Sharing system (PABS): A process to draft and negotiate PABS through
an Intergovernmental Working Group (IGWG) was launched.
5.6.2. UNIVERSAL IMMUNIZATION PROGRAMME (UIP)
WHO/UNICEF 2024 estimates show significant progress on Immunization in India.
Universal Immunization Programme (UIP) of India
• Coverage: Provides free immunization against 12 diseases:
o Diphtheria, Pertussis, Tetanus, Polio, Measles, Rubella, Childhood Tuberculosis, Hepatitis B ,
Meningitis & Pneumonia, Rotavirus diarrhoea, Pneumococcal Pneumonia, and Japanese Encephalitis.
• Under the National Rural Health Mission, the UIP has become a central component of India's public health
efforts.
• Achievements: Polio-Free India and Neonatal tetanus elimination
• U-WIN Platform has been launched for the digitalization of all vaccination services provided under UIP.
5.6.3. MONOCLONAL ANTIBODIES (MABS)
The Indian Council of Medical Research (ICMR) seeks partners to develop monoclonal antibodies (mAbs)
against Nipah viral disease.
• Nipah virus is a zoonotic pathogen causing severe respiratory and neurological illness, transmitted from
bats or infected animals to humans.
About Monoclonal Antibodies (mAbs)
• Definition: Monoclonal antibodies are laboratory-produced proteins, engineered to bind specific antigens,
mimicking natural immune responses.
• Origin: Derived from identical immune cells cloned from a single parent B-cell.
• Function: Used to diagnose, prevent, and treat diseases including cancers, autoimmune disorders, and
viral infections.
5.6.4. 3 BY 35 INITIATIVES
WHO launched 3 By 35 Initiatives, aims to raise prices of any or all of three unhealthy products tobacco,
alcohol, and sugary drinks by at least 50% through health taxes by 2035.
What is Health Tax?
• Levied on products that have a negative public health impact e.g. Tobacco, etc.
• WHO recommends taxation as one of the most cost-effective tools for addressing population levels of
obesity and other related non-communicable diseases (NCDs).
5.6.5. NATIONAL ONE HEALTH MISSION
Union Government will launch National One Health Mission (NOHM).
• One Health is an integrated, unifying approach to balance and optimize the health of people, animals and
the environment.
About National One Health Mission
• A multi-sectorial initiative integrating human, livestock, wildlife and environmental health to strengthen
coordinated surveillance, diagnostics and outbreak response.
• Nodal Agency: Anchored by Indian Council of Medical Research (ICMR) under Office of the Principal
Scientific Adviser (PSA)

• Key Highlights of the Agreement
o Strengthen Pandemic prevention and surveillance: As per International Health Regulations (IHR),
2005.

5.6.2. UNIVERSAL IMMUNIZATION PROGRAMME (UIP)
WHO/UNICEF 2024 estimates show significant progress on Immunization in India.
Universal Immunization Programme (UIP) of India
• Coverage: Provides free immunization against 12 diseases:
o Diphtheria, Pertussis, Tetanus, Polio, Measles, Rubella, Childhood Tuberculosis, Hepatitis B ,
Meningitis & Pneumonia, Rotavirus diarrhoea, Pneumococcal Pneumonia, and Japanese Encephalitis.
• Under the National Rural Health Mission, the UIP has become a central component of India's public health
efforts.
• Achievements: Polio-Free India and Neonatal tetanus elimination
• U-WIN Platform has been launched for the digitalization of all vaccination services provided under UIP.
5.6.3. MONOCLONAL ANTIBODIES (MABS)
The Indian Council of Medical Research (ICMR) seeks partners to develop monoclonal antibodies (mAbs)
against Nipah viral disease.
• Nipah virus is a zoonotic pathogen causing severe respiratory and neurological illness, transmitted from
bats or infected animals to humans.
About Monoclonal Antibodies (mAbs)
• Definition: Monoclonal antibodies are laboratory-produced proteins, engineered to bind specific antigens,
mimicking natural immune responses.
• Origin: Derived from identical immune cells cloned from a single parent B-cell.
• Function: Used to diagnose, prevent, and treat diseases including cancers, autoimmune disorders, and
viral infections.

• Anchor Institution: National Institute of One Health, Nagpur.
• Need of the Mission: Zoonotic Risk Mitigation, Pandemic Preparedness, curbing AMR, etc.
5.6.6. TRANSFATS
The WHO officially recognized four countries (Austria, Norway, Oman and Singapore) for their outstanding
leadership in eliminating industrially produced trans fats.
About Trans fats (Trans-fatty acids (TFA))
• Definition: TFA are unsaturated fatty acids that can be industrially produced (via hydrogenation) or
naturally found in animal-source foods (meat, milk).
o Industrially produced trans fats are commonly found in baked goods like biscuits, margarine, and
Vanaspati ghee, etc.
• Health Impacts:
o Raises the level of the bad cholesterol and low density lipoprotein-cholesterol (LDL-c).
o They have been also linked to inflammation, overweight/obesity, high blood pressure, diabetes and
some types of cancers.
Key Initiative taken
• WHO’s "REPLACE" Action Package
• India enforced limiting trans fats in oils to no more than 2 % through the Food Safety and Standards
(Prohibition and Restrictions on Sales) Second Amendment Regulations, 2021.
5.6.7. WEIGHT LOSS DRUG
Danish pharma company Novo Nordisk launched its weight loss drug Wegovy in India.
• The drug semaglutide, marketed under the brand name Wegovy, will be available in the form of injections.
o Semaglutide, works as an appetite suppressant by imitating a hormone called glucagon-like peptide-
1 (GLP-1).
> GLP-1 is an intestinal hormone released after eating, and usually makes people feel fuller.
• Other weight loss drugs include Ozempic, Mounjaro (made up of tirzepatide (mimics function of GLP-1 and
glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide (GIP)), etc.
5.6.8. ORAL REHYDRATION SALTS (ORS)
Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI) has directed states and UTs to remove beverages and
similar products being marketed using the term ORS.
About ORS
• ORS term is associated exclusively with WHO-recommended Oral Rehydration Salts, which are drug
products.
• Purpose: It is a simple, low-cost glucose electrolyte solution used to prevent and treat dehydration from
acute diarrhoeal diseases.
• WHO recommended ORS salts composition: Sodium chloride, glucose (anhydrous), potassium chloride,
and trisodium citrate (dihydrate).
• Functions: Glucose enhances sodium and water absorption, sodium and potassium replace essential
electrolyte losses, and citrate helps correct dehydration-related acidosis.
• Developed by Dilip Mahalanabis.

5.6.9. TYPES OF DIABETES
Malnutrition-related diabetes has been officially recognized and named “type 5 diabetes” at International
Diabetes Federation’s (IDF) World Diabetes Congress.
• Also called J-type diabetes, is known to be associated with under nutrition and low weight (body mass
index (BMI)).
o Diabetes occurs either when the pancreas does not produce enough insulin or when the body cannot
effectively use the insulin it produces.
• It is a type of diabetes linked to chronic undernutrition, especially during childhood or adolescence.
Other Types of Diabetes
• Type 1: Autoimmune destruction of insulin-producing beta cells that prevents your pancreas from making
insulin.
• Type 2: Mainly caused by obesity and over nutrition (Body does not use insulin well).
• Gestational: Occurs with high blood glucose during pregnancy and can cause complications for both
mother and child.
5.6.10. STEP-AND-SHOOT SPOT-SCANNING PROTON ARC THERAPY (SPARC)
For the first time, SPArc has been used to treat an Adenoid Cystic Carcinoma (ACC), a relatively rare form of
cancer.
About Step-and-Shoot Proton Arc (SPArc)
• A kind of advanced radiation therapy technique (kills cancer cells or slows their growth by damaging their
DNA).
o It works by targeting tumours with protons while sparing surrounding tissue.
• Benefits: Improves the quality of radiation dose planning for various cancers like head and neck, brain, etc.

6. DEFENCE
6.1. AIR DEFENCE SYSTEM (ADS)
6.1.1. INDIA’S AIR DEFENCE SYSTEM (ADS)
Why in the News?
India’s Air Defence System (ADS) successfully thwarted attacks on India’s Western Border.
India’s Air Defence System (ADS)
India’s ADS consists of an Integrated Counter UAS (Unmanned Aerial Systems) Grid, where following
surface-to-air missile (SAM) systems played a key role.-
• S-400 Triumf (Acquired from Russia)
o Also called Sudarshan Chakra in India.
o Equipped with a command-and-control system, phased array radars and electronic warfare
countermeasures.
o Offers complete 360-degree radar and missile coverage.
o Multi-missile compatibility enables layered defence.
• Barak 8 (Jointly developed by India and Israel)
o Medium to long-range (MR SAM or LR SAM)
o Capable of simultaneously engaging multiple targets in the air.
o Both maritime and land-based variants of the system exist.
• Akash Weapon System (Indigenously Built)
o Short Range SAM
o Equipped with built-in Electronic Counter-Counter Measures (ECCM).
o Can simultaneously engage Multiple Targets in Group Mode or Autonomous Mode.
o High immunity against active and passive jamming.
Related News
Akashteer
• It played a pivotal role in neutralising drone and missile threats during border skirmishes with Pakistan.
• About Akashteer
o India’s fully indigenous, automated Air Defence Control and Reporting System.
o Designed to automate detection, tracking and engagement of enemy aircraft, drones and missiles.
o Reflects a strategic shift from passive defence to proactive retaliation.
o Key Features:
> Part of the C4ISR (Command, Control, Communications, Computers, Intelligence,
Surveillance and Reconnaissance) framework.
> Vehicle-based system, which makes it mobile.
> AI-driven and fully automated air defence solution
o Developed by: Bharat Electronics Limited (BEL).
6.1.2. INTEGRATED AIR DEFENCE WEAPON SYSTEM (IADWS)
Why in the News?
DRDO successfully conducted maiden flight-tests of Integrated Air Defence Weapon System (IADWS).
About IADWS
• IADWS is expected to provide multiple layers of protection by integrating surveillance, threat
identification, and air defense systems.
o It will help in detecting and neutralizing threats such as long-range missiles, aircraft, and unmanned
aerial vehicles.

• Command Centre: Integrated operation is controlled by a Centralized Command and Control Centre

Components of IADWS
• Indigenous Quick Reaction Surface to Air Missiles (QRSAM): Developed by DRDO
o A short-range SAM system designed to protect moving armoured columns from aerial attacks.

Imarat (RCI)
o Man Portable Air Defence System (MANPADS) designed to neutralize low-altitude aerial threats at
short ranges.
• A high-power laser-based Directed Energy Weapon (DEW): Developed by Centre for High Energy
Systems and Sciences
Related News
Project Kusha or Program Long Range Surface to Air missile (PGLRSAM)
• An indigenous initiative led by DRDO to develop a long-range air defence missile system.
• Consists of Two Phases:
o Phase 1: Includes 4 interceptors’ variants (under developments: M1, M2, M3 and Naval variant)
o Phase 2: Aims to develop an even longer range (exceeding 600 km) interceptor missile.
Anant Shastra Air Defence System
• Indian Army has awarded a contract to BEL for development and supply of ‘Anant Shastra’ air defense
system.
• About Anant Shastra
o An indigenous Quick Reaction Surface-to-Air Missile (QRSAM) system.
o Developed by: DRDO in collaboration with BEL and Bharat Dynamics Limited (BDL).
o Capabilities: Highly mobile and agile with a range of around 30 km.

• SFC forms part of India’s Nuclear Command Authority and is responsible for the management and
administration of the country's tactical and strategic nuclear weapons stockpile.
About Agni-5
• A Nuclear-capable Surface-to-Surface Ballistic Missile (SSBM) and is powered by a three-stage solid fuel
engine.
• Range: Over 5,000 km
• Developed by: DRDO
• Agni is one of the five missile types developed under the Integrated Guided Missile Development
Programme (IGMDP).
• Significance: Places India among few nations (US, Russia, China, France) with Multiple Independently
Targetable Reentry Vehicle (MIRV)-capable ICBMs.
About IGMDP
• Started in 1983 to attain self-sufficiency in the field of missile technology.
• The missiles developed under the program were
o Prithvi: Short-range surface-to-surface ballistic missile.
o Trishul: Short-range low-level surface-to-air missile.
o Akash: Medium-range surface-to-air missile.
o Nag: Third-generation anti-tank missile.
Agni Prime
• DRDO & Strategic Force Command (SFC) successfully launched the Agni-Prime Missile from a Rail
based Mobile launcher system.
o This is first-of-its-kind futuristic rail-based missile system that can move on rail networks without
pre-conditions.
• About Agni-Prime Missile
o Two-stage, solid-fueled, canister-launched ballistic missile.
> It can carry both conventional and nuclear warheads.
o First of the new generation of missiles after the end of the IGMDP.

More on the News

57 AHMEDABAD | BENGALURU | BHOPAL | CHANDIGARH | DELHI | GUWAHATI | HYDERABAD | JAIPUR | JODHPUR | LUCKNOW | PRAYAGRAJ | PUNE | RANCHI ©Vision IAS
About Scramjet Engine
• A scramjet engine means a Supersonic Combusting
Ramjet engine.
o It is an improvement over the ramjet engine.
> A ramjet is a form of air-breathing jet engine that
uses the vehicle’s forward motion to compress
incoming air for combustion without a rotating
compressor.
• Key Features:
o Uses flame stabilisation technique: This holds
continuous flame inside the combustor.
o Depends on assisted take off: Both ramjets and
scramjets cannot produce thrust at zero air speed.
> Therefore, a scramjet-powered vehicle requires an assisted take off by a rocket to accelerate it to
a speed where it begins to produce thrust.
• India is part of elite club of nations that have successfully tested scramjet engines which includes the USA,
Russia, China, etc.
Advantages of Scramjet Technology
• Increased Efficiency: It is a more efficient propulsion system than a rocket.
o A rocket engine carries both fuel and oxidizer, while a jet engine relies on atmospheric oxygen for
combustion.
• Enhanced Space Access: It will reduce the cost of space missions as jet engines are re-usable.
o Also, rockets fitted with scramjet engines will be able to carry heavier satellites.
o An ISRO project named AVATAR, aimed at developing a rocket to launch the ramjets and scramjets
engine.
6.4. GSAT-7R
Why in the News?
GSAT-7R (CMS-03) Satellite was successfully launched aboard the ISRO’s LVM3 from the Satish Dhawan Space
Centre, Sriharikota.
About GSAT-7R (CMS-03) Satellite Communication
• Part of India’s advanced defence satellite communication series GSAT-7.
• Orbit: Launched into the Geosynchronous Transfer Orbit (GTO), it will eventually settle into Geostationary
Orbit.
• Coverage: Provides multi-band telecommunication services across the Indian Ocean Region and Indian
landmass up to 2,000 kms from India’s coastline.
• Multiple Communication channels: The satellite operates on multiple frequency bands. The system
enables the transmission of voice, video and data.
• Lifespan: 15 years and will replace the multi-band communication satellite GSAT-7/INSAT-4F Rukmini.
• Purpose: Strengthens space-based communication, maritime domain awareness, and secure links among
Navy ships, submarines, aircraft, and Maritime Operations Centres.
Hypersonic Weapon
• Travel at Mach-5 or faster, or at least five times the speed of sound and is powered by Air breathing
engine.
• Fly at lower altitudes than ballistic missiles, making them harder to track.
• Types:
o Hypersonic Glide Vehicles (HGV): Launched from a rocket, similar to regular ballistic missiles,
before gliding to a target.
o Hypersonic Cruise Missiles (HCM): Powered throughout their flight via Scramjet engines.

o It is an improvement over the ramjet engine.
A ramjet is a form of air-breathing jet engine that
uses the vehicle’s forward motion to compress
incoming air for combustion without a rotating
compressor.
• Key Features:
o Uses flame stabilisation technique: This holds
continuous flame inside the combustor.
o Depends on assisted take off: Both ramjets and
scramjets cannot produce thrust at zero air speed.
Therefore, a scramjet-powered vehicle requires an assisted take off by a rocket to accelerate it to
a speed where it begins to produce thrust.
• India is part of elite club of nations that have successfully tested scramjet engines which includes the USA,
Russia, China, etc.
Advantages of Scramjet Technology
• Increased Efficiency: It is a more efficient propulsion system than a rocket.
o A rocket engine carries both fuel and oxidizer, while a jet engine relies on atmospheric oxygen for
combustion.
• Enhanced Space Access: It will reduce the cost of space missions as jet engines are re-usable.
o Also, rockets fitted with scramjet engines will be able to carry heavier satellites.
o An ISRO project named AVATAR, aimed at developing a rocket to launch the ramjets and scramjets
engine.
6.4. GSAT-7R
Why in the News?
GSAT-7R (CMS-03) Satellite was successfully launched aboard the ISRO’s LVM3 from the Satish Dhawan Space
Centre, Sriharikota.
About GSAT-7R (CMS-03) Satellite Communication
• Part of India’s advanced defence satellite communication series GSAT-7.
• Orbit: Launched into the Geosynchronous Transfer Orbit (GTO), it will eventually settle into Geostationary
Orbit.
• Coverage: Provides multi-band telecommunication services across the Indian Ocean Region and Indian
landmass up to 2,000 kms from India’s coastline.
• Multiple Communication channels: The satellite operates on multiple frequency bands. The system
enables the transmission of voice, video and data.
• Lifespan: 15 years and will replace the multi-band communication satellite GSAT-7/INSAT-4F Rukmini.
• Purpose: Strengthens space-based communication, maritime domain awareness, and secure links among
Navy ships, submarines, aircraft, and Maritime Operations Centres.
Hypersonic Weapon
• Travel at Mach-5 or faster, or at least five times the speed of sound and is powered by Air breathing
engine.
• Fly at lower altitudes than ballistic missiles, making them harder to track.
• Types:
o Hypersonic Glide Vehicles (HGV): Launched from a rocket, similar to regular ballistic missiles,
before gliding to a target.
o Hypersonic Cruise Missiles (HCM): Powered throughout their flight via Scramjet engines.

6.5. MK-II (A) LASER- DIRECTED ENERGY WEAPON (DEW) SYSTEM
Why in the News?
DRDO successfully conducted a trial of an Mk-II (A) LASER- Directed Energy Weapon (DEW) system at the
National Open-Air Range (NOAR) in Andhra Pradesh.
More on the News
• India joined group of countries such as US, China, and Russia who possess advanced LASER weapon
capabilities.
• Also, recently, Israel becomes First Country to shoot down enemy Drones with Laser Weapon.
o Israel Air Force's Aerial Defense Array used the prototype laser air defences.
o It is described as an adapted version of the Iron Beam system, a High Energy Laser Weapon System
(HELWS).
About Mk-II (A) DEW system
• Developed indigenously by DRDO’s Centre for High Energy Systems and Sciences (CHESS), Hyderabad.
• Mechanism: Once detected by a radar or inbuilt Electro Optic (EO) system, it engages targets and uses an
intense high-energy 30 kilowatt LASER Beam.
• Characteristics: Lightning speed of engagement, precision, and can have lethality delivered to the target
within a few seconds.
About Directed Energy Weapons
• They use concentrated energy from electromagnetic or particle technology, rather than kinetic energy,
to degrade or destroy targets.
• Types: High Energy LASER (HELs), High power microwave weapons (HPMs), millimeter waves, Particle
beams weapons.
• DEWs generally have a shorter range than conventional weapons, and weather conditions—such as fog and
storms—can make certain DEW less effective.
Other DEW Systems
• USA: High Energy Laser with Integrated Optical-dazzler and Surveillance (HELIOS) system
• China: Hurricane-3000 and Silent Hunter
• UK: DragonFire, Radio Frequency Directed Energy Weapon (RFDEW)
• Russia: Peresvet laser
• India: Integrated Drone Detection and Interdiction System (IDD&IS) Mk2A by DRDO

DRDO conducted maiden flight-trials of Stratospheric Airship Platform (SAP).
More on the News
• It has been developed by Aerial Delivery Research and Development Establishment, Agra.

59 AHMEDABAD | BENGALURU | BHOPAL | CHANDIGARH | DELHI | GUWAHATI | HYDERABAD | JAIPUR | JODHPUR | LUCKNOW | PRAYAGRAJ | PUNE | RANCHI ©Vision IAS
• It will enhance India’s earth observation and Intelligence, Surveillance & Reconnaissance (ISR)
capability.
About the Stratospheric Airship Platform (SAP)
• A high-altitude, unmanned airship designed to operate in the stratosphere, typically between 17 to 22
kilometers above Earth.
• Functions as a High-Altitude Pseudo-Satellite (HAPS) that can remain airborne for extended durations.
o HAPS can be classified as aerodynamic (or heavier-than-air, e.g. fixed wing/airplanes) and aerostatic
(or lighter-than-air, e.g. balloons and airships).
• Lift Mechanism: Uses helium for buoyancy
• Power Source: Generally powered by solar energy with onboard batteries for night-time operation.
• Strategic Advantages: They can carry imaging sensors, radar, and telecom payloads, making them useful
for border monitoring, disaster response, and intelligence missions.
o They bridge the gap between drones and satellites, providing space-like coverage with faster
deployment, lower costs, and more flexibility.
6.7. AIRCRAFT/HELICOPTER
6.7.1. ADVANCED MEDIUM COMBAT AIRCRAFT (AMCA)
Why in the News?
Defence Minister approved Fifth-Generation Fighter Jet Execution Model through Industry Tie-Up.
More on the News
• India will jointly manufacture advanced jet engines for 5th generation fighter jet aircraft named Advanced
Medium Combat Aircraft (AMCA) programme with French aerospace major Safran.
o Currently only the US (F-22 and F-35), Russia (Sukhoi Su-57), and China (Chengdu J-20) have
developed operational fifth generation aircraft.
AMCA Programme Overview
• Lead Agency: Aeronautical Development Agency (ADA) under DRDO.
• Core Capabilities: Supercruise, internal weapons bays, AI-driven avionics, radar-absorbing structures, etc.
• Variants: AMCA Mk1 with GE-F414 engines; Mk2 planned with indigenous engines.
Key Features of 5th Generation Fighters
• Powered by Twin-engine: Provides higher level of air safety and survivability in the event of failure of one
engine especially at night.
• Stealth Capabilities: Harder to detect by enemy radar.
• Other: High maneuverability, fly at supersonic speeds, etc.

o VTOL UAVs can take off and land vertically, eliminating the need for runways, and providing
manoeuvrability and versatility in operations.
• Features:
o Equipped with precision-guided anti-personnel warheads.
o Can relay back live video and return to the launch position in automated mode.
6.9.2. BHARGAVASTRA
India has successfully test-fired an indigenously developed anti-drone system, Bhargavastra.
About Bhargavastra
• A low-cost, 'hard kill' anti-drone system designed to neutralize threats from drone swarms.
• Two-layer security system:
o Layer 1: Unguided micro-rockets with a 20m lethal radius for swarm drones
o Layer 2: Guided micro-missiles for precision strikes
6.9.3. LONG-RANGE GLIDE BOMB 'GAURAV'
DRDO successfully conducts release trials of indigenously developed Long-Range Glide Bomb 'Gaurav'.
• Glide bomb after launch steer towards the target using highly accurate hybrid navigation scheme with a
combination of Indian Navigation System (INS) and Global Positioning System (GPS) data.
About Gaurav
• Weight: 1,000 kg class
• Range: ‘Gaurav’ achieved 100 km range with pinpoint accuracy.
6.9.4. FIBER OPTIC DRONES
Recently used in the Russia-Ukraine conflict, fiber optic drones are seen as a game-changer in drone warfare
due to their resistance to electronic warfare (EW).
About Fiber Optic Drones
• Operated using long fiber optic cables instead of radio links.
o Fiber optic cable is made up of a collection of glass tubes spun as thin as hairs.
o They can support faster speeds, longer distances, and simultaneous communications.
o A single fiber can carry multiple signals simultaneously on different wavelengths
• No radio link means they cannot be jammed or intercepted by EW systems.
6.9.5. PROJECT 17A
Two multi-mission stealth frigates of Project 17A – INS Udaygiri and INS Himgiri, were commissioned into the
Indian Navy.
About Project 17 A
• Follow-on ships of Shivalik-class (Project 17) frigates.
o Under it, Seven Frigates being indigenously built by Mazagon Dock (Mumbai) and GRSE (Kolkata).
• Configured with Combined Diesel or Gas (CODOG) main propulsion plants, comprising a Diesel Engine
and Gas turbine.

7. ALTERNATIVE ENERGY
7.1. NUCLEAR ENERGY
7.1.1. TOKAMAK REACTORS
Why in the News?
World’s biggest nuclear fusion project, ITER has completed its central magnet system, with India playing a
critical role in building several key components.
More on the News
• India has manufactured cryostat, an enormous chamber that houses the entire ITER Tokamak.
• India has also built cryolines that carry liquid helium to cool magnets.
About ITER
• Objective: To demonstrate the viability of fusion as an abundant, safe, carbon-free energy source.
• Located in France.
• India’s Membership: Institute for Plasma Research in Ahmedabad is lead institution from India
participating in the project.

About Tokamak Reactor
• Purpose: Designed to harness the energy of fusion.
• Working: In it, fusion plasma is created and confined by strong magnetic fields.
o Plasma is a fundamental state of matter along with solids, liquids and gases.
• Other Major Tokamak Reactor: Joint European Torus (JET) in UK, Experimental Advanced
Superconducting Tokamak (EAST) in China, Korea Superconducting Tokamak Advanced Research
(KSTAR) in South Korea, JT-60SA (built and operated by Japan and Europe in Japan).
India’s activities in nuclear fusion and Tokamak Research
• ADITYA: First indigenously designed and built tokamak by Institute of plasma research.
• ADITYA-U Tokamak: A medium-size air-core tokamak.
• Steadystate Superconducting Tokamak-1 (SST-1): Medium size superconducting tokamak
7.1.2. FAST BREEDER REACTOR
Why in the News?
India’s first Fast-Breeder Nuclear Reactor (500 MWe) is set for commissioning by 2026.
About Fast Breeder Reactor
• Genesis: Government established Bharatiya Nabhikiya Vidyut Nigam Limited (BHAVINI) to construct and
operate Prototype Fast Breeder Reactor (PFBR).
• Functioning:
o Uses indigenously produced Uranium-Plutonium Mixed Oxide (MOX) fuel and utilize liquid sodium as
a coolant.
o Uranium-238 blanket surrounding fuel core will undergo nuclear transmutation to produce more fuel,
thus earning the name ‘Breeder’.
• Location: Kalpakkam, Tamil Nadu
Significance of FBR
• Technological Advancement: Once commissioned, India will only be the second country after Russia to
have commercial operating FBR.
• Utilization of Thorium Reserve: The operationalization of PFBR will mark the start of stage II of India’s
three-stage nuclear power program.
o By transmutation, Thorium will create fissile U-233 which will be used as fuel in the third stage.
• Safety: passive safety features ensuring a prompt and safe shut down of the plant in the event of an
emergency.

Related News
Mahi Banswara Rajasthan Atomic Power Project (MBRAPP)
• PM laid the foundation of the Mahi Banswara Rajasthan Atomic Power Project (MBRAPP).
• About MBRAPP Project
o Capacity: 4 x 700 MWe PHWR (Pressurized Heavy Water Reactor)
o It will be developed by Anushakti Vidhyut Nigam (ASHVINI).
> ASHVINI is a joint venture between Nuclear Power Corporation India Limited (NPCIL) and
National Thermal Power Corporation Limited (NTPC).
o The project is part of India’s “fleet mode” initiative.
> Under this Initiative, ten identical 700 MW reactors are being built across India under uniform
design and procurement plans.
Enrichment of uranium
• Recently, concerns have been raised on Iran’s resumption of enrichment of uranium.
• About Enrichment of uranium
o To enrich uranium means taking the naturally found element and increasing the proportion of
uranium-235 while removing uranium-238.
o Method: Mainly done using centrifuges.
o Enrichment Levels & Uses
> (3–5%) U-235: Civilian nuclear power reactors.
> (>20%) U-235: Highly enriched uranium (HEU), potential for weapons.
> (~90%) U-235: Weapons-grade, compact and efficient for nuclear arms.
7.1.3. THORIUM MOLTEN SALT REACTOR (TMSR)
Why in the News?
China successfully achieved the first-ever thorium to uranium fuel conversion in a Thorium Molten Salt Reactor
(TMSR)

(3–5%) U-235: Civilian nuclear power reactors.
(>20%) U-235: Highly enriched uranium (HEU), potential for weapons.
(~90%) U-235: Weapons-grade, compact and efficient for nuclear arms.
7.1.3. THORIUM MOLTEN SALT REACTOR (TMSR)
Why in the News?
China successfully achieved the first-ever thorium to uranium fuel conversion in a Thorium Molten Salt Reactor
(TMSR)

66
© Vision IAS AHMEDABAD | BENGALURU | BHOPAL | CHANDIGARH | DELHI | GUWAHATI | HYDERABAD | JAIPUR | JODHPUR | LUCKNOW | PRAYAGRAJ | PUNE | RANCHI
About Thorium Molten Salt Reactor (TMSR)
• It is fourth-generation advanced nuclear energy system that uses high-temperature molten salt as a
coolant.
o China’s TMSR is currently the only operational molten-salt reactor in the world.
• Key Features: Cool without water, run at atmospheric pressure, deliver a high-temperature output, etc.
• Key Advantages:
o Passive Safety Features: If an MSR is too hot, a salt plug melts, stopping the reaction.
o Small High-Level Nuclear Waste Footprint: Thorium generates fewer long-lived minor actinides
(radioactive metallic elements) than plutonium fuels.
o Other: Extract more energy from fuel, etc.
Thorium Fuel
• Thorium has to be converted to Uranium-233 in a reactor before it can be used as fuel.
o Thorium-232 is the only naturally occurring isotope of thorium.
• It is three times more abundant in nature than uranium.
o Despite its widespread availability, the metal’s utilisation is restricted by its high extraction cost.
• Advanced Heavy Water Reactor, currently under development with Bhabha Atomic Research Centre
(BARC), would serve as a technology demonstrator for thorium fuel cycle.
o Also, India is developing an Indian Molten Salt Breeder Reactor (IMSBR).
Thorium reserves in India
• India has the world’s largest reserves of thorium.
• Kerala and Odisha have rich reserves of monazite, which contains about 8 – 10% thorium.
• It is also found in Andhra Pradesh, Tamil Nadu, West Bengal and Jharkhand.
7.2. LITHIUM-ION (LI-ION)
Why in the News?
India’s Largest Lithium-ion (Li-ion) Battery (LIB) manufacturing plant was inaugurated in Haryana.
About Li-ion Battery
• It is a type of rechargeable battery.
• In it, lithium ions move between a negative electrode (graphite) and a positive electrode (Li transitional
metal oxides) through a non-aqueous electrolyte during charging and discharging.
Benefits of Lithium-ion Batteries
• High energy density: Stores more energy compactly and offers longer cycles between charges.
• Lightweight and Lower Toxic Heavy Metals: Compared to older lead-acid batteries, it is much lighter due
to use of less toxic and lightweight Li and carbon electrodes.
• Excellent performance: They provide good cycle stability, efficiency, and reliability, with low self-
discharge and generally no memory effect (slow-down due to repeated charging).
Related News
Black Mass Recovery Technology
• Technology Development Board (TDB) has extended financial support for the commercialization of an
indigenous battery recycling technology that utilizes dual-mode (wet and dry) black mass recovery.
• What is Black Mass?
o When a lithium-ion battery reaches the end of its life, it doesn’t stop being valuable. Inside that used
battery is a dark, powdery substance known as black mass — a mix of critical minerals.
o This can be recovered, refined, and reused to power the next generation of clean energy.
o Black mass recovery technology: It is a process that extracts valuable materials like lithium, cobalt,
and nickel from used lithium-ion batteries.

7.3. VANADIUM REDOX FLOW BATTERY
Why in the News?
India’s largest and first MWh-Scale Vanadium Redox Flow Battery (VRFB) inaugurated at NTPC NETRA.
More on the News
• Flow Batteries, along with solid state batteries, are one of the next generation battery technologies and are
emerging as an alternative to lithium-ion for grid-scale applications.
About Next Generation Battery Technologies
• Flow Batteries: Flow batteries are powered by reduction-oxidation (redox) reactions and involve two
different liquid electrolytes that pass ions or protons back and forth through a porous membrane.
o These batteries can store larger amounts of energy—as much as the size of the electrolyte cells can
contain—and don’t use flammable or polluting materials.
o Types of Flow Batteries: Vanadium Redox, Zinc–Bromine, Iron–Salt/Organic systems.
• Solid-State Batteries: These use solid electrolyte solutions, which don’t need a different separator.
o SSBs have several advantages over conventional batteries
> Improved safety where solid electrolytes are less prone to leakage or combustion compared to
liquid electrolytes,
> Potential to store more energy in the same volume or weight
> Capable of recharging up to 4–6 times faster than liquid electrolyte batteries, etc.
Sodium-Ion Battery (SIB)
• Research team working under Department of Science and Technology has developed a super-fast
charging sodium-ion battery (SIB) that can charge up to 80% in just six minutes and last over 3000
charge cycles.
• About Sodium-Ion Battery (SIB)
o SIBs are a type of rechargeable battery similar to lithium batteries, but carry the charge using
sodium ions (Na+) instead of lithium ions (Li+).
o Advantages of Sodium-Ion Batteries (SIBs) Compared to LIBs:
> Energy density: SIBs generally have lower energy density than Li-ion
> Safety: SIBs can be transported at zero voltage (fully discharged), reducing fire risks.

8. MISCELLANEOUS
8.1. GEOSPATIAL TECHNOLOGY
8.1.1. OPERATION DRONAGIRI
Why in the News?
Haryana has constituted state and district-level committees for the implementation of Operation Dronagiri.
About Operation Dronagiri
• A pilot project under National Geospatial Policy 2022, and will be implemented by Geospatial Innovation
Cell, Department of Science and Technology.
• Purpose: To demonstrate the potential applications of geospatial technologies and innovations in improving
the quality of life of citizens and ease of doing business.
• First Phase Implementation: In the states of UP, Haryana, Assam, Andhra Pradesh & Maharashtra.
• Focus is on Agriculture, Livelihoods, Logistics and Transport , Urban Planning and Infrastructure.
• development of an Integrated Geospatial Data Sharing Interface (GDI)
National Geospatial Policy 2022
• Vision: Position India as global leader in geospatial sector, develop national framework, and enable
availability of geospatial data.
• Institutional Framework: Policy provided for establishment of Geospatial Data Promotion and
Development Committee as apex body for formulating guidelines and programs for promotion of
Geospatial sector.
• Nodal Agency: Survey of India (SoI)

Other key Initiatives taken to Promote Geospatial sector
• National Geospatial Mission: Aims to develop foundational geospatial infrastructure
• National Geospatial Data Repository: Serves as a centralized platform for geospatial data management
and access.
• Continuously Operating Reference Stations (CORS) Network: Launched by SoI
8.2. CRITICAL MINERALS
Why in the News?
The Quad (United States, Japan, India, and Australia) launched the Critical Minerals Initiative.
What are Critical Minerals?
• Critical minerals are naturally occurring elements or compounds.
o These are classified as critical due to high demand (diverse applications), supply risks, etc.
• Ministry of Mines has released a list of 30 critical minerals.
o These minerals are - Antimony, Beryllium, Bismuth, Cobalt, Copper, Gallium, Germanium, Graphite,
Hafnium, Indium, Lithium, Molybdenum, Niobium, Nickel, PGE, Phosphorous, Potash, Rare Earth
Elements (REEs), Rhenium, Silicon, Strontium, Tantalum, Tellurium, Tin, Titanium, Tungsten, Vanadium,
Zirconium, Selenium, and Cadmium.
Other initiatives taken to secure Critical Minerals supply
• India
o Mines and Minerals (Development and Regulation) Amendment Act, 2023, enables exploration and
mining of critical minerals.
o National Critical Mineral Mission (NCMM)
o National Mineral Policy, 2019
• International Collaborations & Agreements
o Minerals Security Partnership (MSP): India joined this US-led initiative in 2023.
o Khanij Bidesh India Limited (KABIL): A joint venture company of Ministry of Mines to acquire critical
minerals globally.
Related News
Nickel-Copper-Platinum Group Element (Ni-Cu-PGE) Sulphide
• India’s first ever discovery of Nickel-Copper-Platinum Group Element (Ni-Cu-PGE) sulphide at
Bhalukona-Jamnidih block of the Mahasamund district in Chhattisgarh.
• Applications of Ni-Cu-PGE
o Nickel: Used in stainless steel, alloys, and batteries (especially for EVs).
o Copper: Electrical wiring, electronics, plumbing, construction.
o PGEs: Catalysts, jewellery, electronics, and hydrogen fuel cell technology.
• Strategic Value: Critical for clean energy technologies, EVs, and defence applications, making it a key
mineral group for energy transition.
8.2.1. RARE EARTH ELEMENTS (REES)
Why in the News?
In retaliation to US tariffs, China restricts exports of Rare Earth Elements (REEs).
What are REEs?
• REEs are a set of 17 lustrous, silvery-white, soft heavy metals in periodic table, e.g., Cerium (Ce), Yttrium
(Y), Europium (Eu), etc.
o Though found abundantly, however, they are difficult to extract as they are not found in concentrated
form.

• Potential Applications:
o Electronics and Electricals: E.g., used in television

have phosphorescent properties.
o Renewable Energy and Green Technologies: REE
alloys prevent corrosion in metals and increase
efficiency in energy storage.
o Rare Earth Magnets: REE magnets have high
remanence and coercivity.
o Other: Defence (e.g., radars), industrial catalyst, etc.
• China dominates with more than 2/3rd of global
production and controls more than 85% of the global
supply chain.
Distribution of REE in India
• India’s REE reserves are mostly found in monazite sands.
• Coastal states have the most rare earth deposits, namely Tamil Nadu, Kerala, Andhra Pradesh, and
Odisha. (2023 Indian Minerals Yearbook).

71 AHMEDABAD | BENGALURU | BHOPAL | CHANDIGARH | DELHI | GUWAHATI | HYDERABAD | JAIPUR | JODHPUR | LUCKNOW | PRAYAGRAJ | PUNE | RANCHI ©Vision IAS
8.3.1. NANOZYME
Researchers at Indian Institute of Science (IISc) developed an artificial metal-based nanozyme (vanadium
pentoxide (V2O5)) to clamp down on abnormal blood clotting caused by conditions like pulmonary
thromboembolism (PTE).
• Under normal conditions, an injured blood vessel witnesses the activation of platelets to form clots while
under PTE overactivation of platelets occur causing excess blood clots or thrombosis leading to
mortality.
About Nanozymes
• They are nanomaterials with intrinsic enzyme-like properties.
• Compared to natural enzymes, they exhibit the unique advantages including high catalytic activity, low
cost, high stability, easy mass production, etc.
8.3.2. NANO FERTILIZERS
Indian Farmers Fertilizer Cooperative Limited (IFFCO) to set up its first overseas nano fertiliser plant in Brazil.
• IFFCO had launched world's first 'Nano Liquid Urea' fertiliser in 2021 & then Nano-DAP in 2023.
About Nano Fertilizers
• Nutrients encapsulated or coated within nanomaterial (measuring 100 nanometres or less).
• Enables controlled release and its subsequent slow diffusion into the soil.
8.3.3. NANO SULPHUR
TERI scientists have developed nano sulphur which reportedly increased mustard yield by 30-40%.
About TERI’s Nano-sulphur
• It is a completely green product that uses biological agents like plant promoting bacteria that secrete
enzymes and metabolites.
o This makes it different from other nono-fertilizers such as nano urea.
• Nano-sulphur has antibacterial and insecticidal properties.
8.4. METAL–ORGANIC FRAMEWORK (MOF)
Why in the News?
Susumu Kitagawa, Richard Robson and Omar M. Yaghi are awarded the Nobel Prize in Chemistry 2025 for the
development of metal–organic frameworks (MOF).
What is Metal–Organic Framework (MOF)?
• MOF is special material made up of two parts:
o Metal ions (e.g., copper, etc.) – this act like the
“joints” or “connecting points”
o Organic molecules (carbon based molecules like
coal, etc.) – these act like the “links” or “bridges” that
connect the metal parts together.
• They join through chemical bonds to form a network
structure.
o Structure can be in one, two, or three dimensions with
a lot of empty spaces or cavities between them.
• The cavities make the material highly porous- full of tiny
holes inside (see infographic).

• Origin of the Idea of MOF: The idea to create MOFs came from studying the structure of diamond.
Key Features
• MOFs can be customized to absorb or trap
specific kinds of materials like gases, water, or
chemicals.
• MOFs are more absorbent than many other
porous materials (like zeolites).
• They act like molecular storehouses, storing
gases, liquids, or other materials.
Key Applications
• Water collection from dry air: MOFs can
capture water vapour from desert air.
• Food Processing: MOF based packaging materials can store the ethylene gas (a plant hormone) into its
cavity, reducing the concentration of ethylene and increases the shelf life.
• Pollution control: Extracting pollutants, such as separating PFAS, antibiotics etc. from polluted water.
• Rare-Earth Elements: Experimentally mining rare-earth elements from wastewater.
• Gas storage: Storing gases such as hydrogen, carbon dioxide, methane etc.
8.5. ELECTRIC VEHICLES (EVS)
Why in the news?
NITI Aayog launched first India Electric Mobility Index.
About India Electric Mobility Index (IEMI)
• First-of-its-kind tool to benchmark states’ progress in e-mobility, was launched in partnership with World
Resources Institute India.
• Tracks 16 performance indicators under 3 thematic categories-Transport Electrification Progress;
Charging Infrastructure Readiness; EV Research & Innovation.
• Groups states into 4 categories: Achievers, Front Runners, Performers, and Aspirants.
o For 2024, Delhi topped with a score of 77, followed by Maharashtra (68).
About Electric Vehicles (EVs)
• Types: Battery EVs (entirely battery-powered); Hybrid EVs (have both engine and electric motor); Fuel Cell
EVs (utilize 'fuel cell technology' to generate electricity for propulsion); Plug-in Hybrid EV (uses both an
engine and a rechargeable battery) etc.
o Main Components of BEV: Electric motor, Inverter, Battery, Control Module, Drive train etc.
o Working Principle: Power for electric motor is converted from DC Battery to AC.
Note: Alternative powertrains include propulsion systems that are not based exclusively on the popular internal
combustion engine. E.g., full battery electrics, hydrogen fuel cells, etc.
Other recent steps taken by India to boost EV adoption
• PM E-DRIVE Scheme: Central Sector scheme supporting e-2Ws, e-3Ws, e-trucks, e-buses, e-
ambulances, and charging infrastructure.
• PM e-Bus Sewa – PSM (Payment Security Mechanism) Scheme: Deployment of 38,000+ e-buses.
• PLI – Advanced Chemistry Cell: Supports 50 GWh domestic battery manufacturing
• EVolutionS program: Launched by Department of Science & Technology (DST) for EV startups.
• Other: Promoting Acoustic Vehicle Alerting System (AVAS), PLI Scheme for Automobile & Auto
Components, etc.

• Food Processing: MOF based packaging materials can store the ethylene gas (a plant hormone) into its
cavity, reducing the concentration of ethylene and increases the shelf life.
• Pollution control: Extracting pollutants, such as separating PFAS, antibiotics etc. from polluted water.
• Rare-Earth Elements: Experimentally mining rare-earth elements from wastewater.
• Gas storage: Storing gases such as hydrogen, carbon dioxide, methane etc.
8.5. ELECTRIC VEHICLES (EVS)
Why in the news?
NITI Aayog launched first India Electric Mobility Index.
About India Electric Mobility Index (IEMI)
• First-of-its-kind tool to benchmark states’ progress in e-mobility, was launched in partnership with World
Resources Institute India.
• Tracks 16 performance indicators under 3 thematic categories-Transport Electrification Progress;
Charging Infrastructure Readiness; EV Research & Innovation.
• Groups states into 4 categories: Achievers, Front Runners, Performers, and Aspirants.
o For 2024, Delhi topped with a score of 77, followed by Maharashtra (68).
About Electric Vehicles (EVs)
• Types: Battery EVs (entirely battery-powered); Hybrid EVs (have both engine and electric motor); Fuel Cell
EVs (utilize 'fuel cell technology' to generate electricity for propulsion); Plug-in Hybrid EV (uses both an
engine and a rechargeable battery) etc.
o Main Components of BEV: Electric motor, Inverter, Battery, Control Module, Drive train etc.
o Working Principle: Power for electric motor is converted from DC Battery to AC.
Note: Alternative powertrains include propulsion systems that are not based exclusively on the popular internal
combustion engine. E.g., full battery electrics, hydrogen fuel cells, etc.
Other recent steps taken by India to boost EV adoption
• PM E-DRIVE Scheme: Central Sector scheme supporting e-2Ws, e-3Ws, e-trucks, e-buses, eambulances, and charging infrastructure.
• PM e-Bus Sewa – PSM (Payment Security Mechanism) Scheme: Deployment of 38,000+ e-buses.
• PLI – Advanced Chemistry Cell: Supports 50 GWh domestic battery manufacturing
• EVolutionS program: Launched by Department of Science & Technology (DST) for EV startups.
• Other: Promoting Acoustic Vehicle Alerting System (AVAS), PLI Scheme for Automobile & Auto
Components, etc.

73 AHMEDABAD | BENGALURU | BHOPAL | CHANDIGARH | DELHI | GUWAHATI | HYDERABAD | JAIPUR | JODHPUR | LUCKNOW | PRAYAGRAJ | PUNE | RANCHI ©Vision IAS
Related News
Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) Technology
• Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB) and IIT Bombay have initiated a pilot project to explore the
implementation of Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) technology.
• About V2G Technology
o V2G refers to technologies that enable EV batteries to send power back to the grid.
> When an EV is not in use, it can act as a decentralised battery energy storage device.
o By incorporating V2G technologies, EVs offer an opportunity to facilitate the integration of
Renewable Energy (RE) and support a demand response market.
o It can also help modulate power in an EV battery to reduce the impact of variable RE on the grid
and improve grid stability.
8.6. HYPERLOOP MOBILITY SYSTEM
Why in the News?
Maharashtra inks deal with IIT Madras startup (TuTr Hyperloop Pvt Ltd) to build hyperloop.
More on the News
• The proposed Linear Induction Motor (LIM)-
based hyperloop mobility system will connect
Jawaharlal Nehru Port Trust in Navi Mumbai to
the upcoming Vadhavan Port.
About Hyperloop Mobility System
• Concept: It is basically a magnetic levitation
(maglev) system where pods travel at ultra-high
speeds through low-pressure tubes.
• Its Functioning and Key Components
o Hyperloop functions in a sealed tube with
minimal air resistance, utilizing vacuums
and magnetic levitation for hovering.
o Linear Induction Motors (LIM) propels pods silently enabling the theoretical speed of 1,200 km/h.
• Key components: Steel tubes, pressurized capsules, a compressor for airflow, and air bearing suspension.
• Advantages: Ultra-high speeds (e.g., Mumbai-Pune in 25 mins), energy efficiency (potentially carbon-free),
noise reduction, and logistics redefinition (moving cargo quickly and efficiently).
8.7. DEEP OCEAN MISSION (DOM)
Why in the News?
Samudrayaan Mission will be launched by 2026 end as per the National Institute of Ocean Technology
(NIOT).
More on the News
• Recently, Two Indian aquanauts dived to depths of 4,025 meters and 5,002 meters in the Atlantic Ocean,
placing India among a select group of nations capable of such deep-sea exploration.
o The dives, conducted aboard the French submersible Nautile in collaboration with IFREMER, France’s
marine research institute, were led by team from NIOT, Chennai.
o The dive is a prelude to India’s Samudrayaan Mission.
• So far, only the U.S., Russia, China, France and Japan have carried out successful deep-ocean crewed
missions.

Samudrayaan Mission
• A component of Deep Ocean Mission (DOM).
• Objective: Explore Ocean at 6,000-metre depth using manned submersible vehicle 'Matsya'.
About Matsya-6000
• 4th Generation self-propelled deep-ocean human scientific submersible vehicle.
• Being developed by NIOT.
• Endurance of 12 hours under normal operation and 96 hours in case of emergency.
About DOM
• Nodal Ministry: Ministry of Earth Sciences
• Purpose: Explore Deep Ocean for resources and develop deep sea technologies for sustainable use of
ocean resources, thus supporting India’s Blue Economy initiatives.
8.8. RESEARCH DEVELOPMENT AND INNOVATION (RDI) SCHEME
Why in the News?
Research Development and Innovation (RDI) Scheme has been launched.
Key Highlights of the Scheme
• Aim: Provide long-term financing or refinancing with long tenors at low/nil interest rates to spur private
investments.
• Nodal Department: Department of Science and Technology
• Fund allocation: ₹1 lakh crore
• Key Objectives
o Encourage private sector to scale up RDI in sunrise domains and sectors relevant for economic security,
strategic purpose, and self-reliance
o Finance transformative projects at higher Technology Readiness Levels, etc.
• Two-Tiered Funding Mechanism
o Special Purpose Fund (SPF): Established within the Anusandhan National Research Foundation
(ANRF), acting as the custodian of funds.
> ANRF was established under the ANRF 2023 Act.
o Second Level Fund Managers: Funds from SPF are allocated here, in the form of long-term
concessional loans or equity funding (for startups).
8.9. OTHER IMPORTANT NEWS
8.9.1. SODAR SYSTEM
SODAR (Sound Detection and Ranging) system facility has been inaugurated at India Meteorological
Department (IMD), Delhi.

pages.rawText:
75 AHMEDABAD | BENGALURU | BHOPAL | CHANDIGARH | DELHI | GUWAHATI | HYDERABAD | JAIPUR | JODHPUR | LUCKNOW | PRAYAGRAJ | PUNE | RANCHI ©Vision IAS
• It is designed & developed by CSIR–Advanced Materials and Processes Research Institute (AMPRI), Bhopal.
About SODAR
• Function: SODAR works by sending sound pulses and subsequently monitoring the intensity and frequency
of the returned signal.
o Specifically, it determines the lower-atmosphere wind profile and temperature structure.
• Provides information on height of inversion, height of fog layer, strength of turbulence etc.
8.9.2. HANSA-3 (NG)
CSIR (Council of Scientific and Industrial Research)-National Aerospace Laboratories (NAL) signs
technology transfer deal with a private firm to manufacture HANSA trainer-planes.
About HANSA- 3 (NG)
• Powered by an advanced fuel-efficient Rotax 912 iSc3 Sports engine.
• Significance: Offers Large and world-class flying training ecosystem; strengthen India’s aviation industry,
etc.
8.9.3. KAVACH
Union Minister for Railways announced that Kavach 5.0 will be implemented to upgrade Mumbai’s suburban
train travel.
About Kavach
• Indigenously Automatic Train Protection system designed to improve train safety and efficiency.
• Function: The Kavach assists loco pilots by displaying signals in the train and automatically applying the
brakes if the pilot fails to do so.
• Ensures that the train operates smoothly even in extreme weather conditions, such as fog.
• Radio Frequency Identification (RFID) tags are placed throughout the entire track length to enable the
Kavach to track train positions and detect any potential safety issues along the route.
8.9.4. SHINKANSEN TECHNOLOGY
Next generation E10 Shinkansen trains are to be introduced simultaneously in India (Mumbai-Ahmedabad
Bullet train project) and Japan.
About Shinkansen Technology
• It is Japan’s High-speed bullet train.
o It is the world's safest and most reliable high-speed, high-volume and high-frequency transport
system.
• Key Features:
o Dedicated track for high-speed rail that is free of level crossings and ATC system (Automatic Train
Control System)
o Aerodynamic optimization of the leading shape, vehicle noise reduction, etc.
8.9.5. BLACK BOX
Aircraft Accident Investigation Bureau (AAIB) recovered the Black Box from the wreckage of Air India’s Boeing
787.
About Black Box
• Purpose: Records data during flights and crucial information on plane crashes.
• Comprise two Critical Flight Recording Devices: Flight Data Recorder and Cockpit Voice Recorder.
• Colour: Bright orange to ensure high visibility.

• Material: Made of strong substances (steel or titanium) and are insulated from extreme heat or cold.
8.9.6. ALTERMAGNETS
Scientists from S.N. Bose National Centre for Basic Sciences (SNBNCBS) discovered a unique duality in
alternemagnets using chromium antimonide (CrSb).
About Altermagnets
• Definition: Altermagnets are a new type of magnetic material that combine the best features of
ferromagnets and antiferromagnets.
o Ferromagnets: Spins align in the same direction, resulting in strong magnetism.
o Antiferromagnets: Spins alternate, thus cancelling each other out, resulting in no external magnetism.
• Features:
o Like antiferromagnets, they exhibit alternating spins and therefore have no net magnetism.
o But due to their unique crystal symmetry, they show intense internal spin polarisation, similar to
ferromagnets.
• CrSb (Chromium Antimonide) shows the strongest spin-splitting among known altermagnets.
8.9.7. ACCELEROMETER
Google's Android Earthquake Alert system has detected over 18,000 earthquakes using phone accelerometer
signals.
About Accelerometer
• A sensing device that measures acceleration and detects the frequency and intensity of human
movement.
• Principle of Functioning: Accelerometers work according to Newton’s law of motion, the force on an object
is equal to its mass times its acceleration.
• Can measure both: Static acceleration (due to gravity) & dynamic acceleration (car crash).
• Key Applications
o Digital Devices: smartphones, Laptops, digital cameras- for rotating the display
o Vehicles: Used to trigger airbags
o Drones: to stabilize their orientation mid-flight
8.9.8. DESALINATION TECHNOLOGIES
IIT Bombay Scientists Developed a new hydrophobic Graphene-based lotus leaf-like solar evaporators that
can facilitate water desalination.
Key Desalination Technologies
• Thermal Technology
o Heating of saline water and collecting the condensed vapor (distillate) to produce pure water.
o Usage: Mainly for seawater desalination
o E.g., Low Temperature Thermal Desalination (LTTD) plants, Lakshadweep
• Membrane Technology
o Feedwater is pumped through semi-permeable membranes to filter out the dissolved solids.
o Usage: Mainly for brackish water desalination
o E.g., Nemmeli Seawater Desalination Plant, Tamil Nadu, on Reverse Osmosis. (Largest desalination
plant in the South Asia)
8.9.9. ADVANCED DRIVER ASSISTANCE SYSTEM
Union Government is considering mandating Advanced Driver Assistance System (ADAS) in all new models
of trucks and buses, including school buses.

77 AHMEDABAD | BENGALURU | BHOPAL | CHANDIGARH | DELHI | GUWAHATI | HYDERABAD | JAIPUR | JODHPUR | LUCKNOW | PRAYAGRAJ | PUNE | RANCHI ©Vision IAS
About ADAS
• A built-in suite of coordinated technologies that help drivers operate their vehicles more safely and avoid
human errors.
• In ADAS, sensors such as Lidar, Radar, and cameras work together to inform features such as pedestrian
detection, and lane departure warning.
• Includes a range of features including Adaptive Cruise Control, Automatic Emergency Braking, Blind Spot
Detection, Cross-Traffic Alert, Parking Assistance, among others.
8.9.10. BIOSTIMULANT
Minister of Agriculture and Farmers' Welfare has withdrawn approval for the sale of 11 biostimulants derived
from animal sources (such as chicken feathers, pig tissue etc.).
About Biostimulant
• Definition: Substances and/or microorganisms whose function when applied to plants or the rhizosphere
is to stimulate natural processes to benefit crop quality, independently of its nutrient content.
o Unlike fertilisers, it does not supply nutrients directly, and unlike pesticides, it does not control pests.
• Popular Biostimulants: Humic acids, seaweed extracts, liquid manure composting and beneficial bacteria
and fungi.
• They are regulated under Fertiliser (Inorganic, Organic or Mixed) (Control) Order, 1985.
• Biostimulants can improve Nutrient availability and use Efficiency, tolerance to stress, etc.
8.8.11. AGRIENICS PROGRAMME
MeitY announced the transfer of technology under the Agrienics Programme.
About Agrienics Programme
• A national programme of MeitY involving research, development, deployment, demonstration, and
commercialization of technologies in agriculture and environment domain.
• Nodal Agency: Centre for Development of Advanced Computing (C-DAC), Kolkata.
8.8.12. METRE CONVENTION
The Department of Consumer Affairs commemorated World Metrology Day 2025, marking the 150th
anniversary of the Metre Convention.
About Metre Convention, 1875
• Purpose: Includes convention and its annexed regulation to assure the international unification and
improvement of the metric system.
o Created the International Bureau of Weights and Measures (BIPM) to develop and maintain a
universal measurement system.
• Significance: International collaboration in metrology, global uniformity of measurements for research,
trade, etc.
• India is a member.
